title: Listening - Part 4: Living in Unusual Homes
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five people describing their experiences living in non-traditional housing

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d always dreamed of living in a lighthouse, but [21]the reality of maintaining such an old, exposed structure is a constant battle against the elements[/21]. I spend most of my weekends scraping salt off the windows and repainting the exterior. It’s a lot of work for one person. However, [26]the panoramic views of the ocean during a storm provide a sense of perspective that makes everything worth it[/26]. You realize how small your problems are compared to the power of the sea. It’s a solitary existence, especially in the winter months when the mist rolls in, but the silence is exactly what I needed after years in the city. || Speaker 2: Moving into a converted shipping container was a bold choice, especially with a young family. [22]We did it primarily because we were priced out of the traditional housing market in our area[/22]. It was a way to own our home without a lifetime of debt. It’s compact, to say the least! [27]The biggest adjustment was having to strictly limit our physical possessions to the bare essentials[/27]. You can't have 'stuff' when your living room is eight feet wide. It’s forced us to become much more intentional about what we buy and keep, which has actually been quite liberating for our family dynamics. || Speaker 3: I’ve been living in an underground 'earthship' for three years now. [23]My main goal was to live entirely off-grid and prove that a zero-waste lifestyle is possible[/23]. It’s built from recycled tires and glass bottles. People think it’s a cave, but it’s actually incredibly bright and warm. [28]What I find most challenging is the skeptical and sometimes judgmental attitude of the local planning authorities[/28]. They just don't know what to make of it. Every time I want to add a solar panel or change a pipe, I have to go through months of bureaucracy. It’s frustrating when you’re trying to do something positive for the environment. || Speaker 4: Living in a converted school bus has allowed me to travel across the country while still having my own bed every night. [24]I chose this lifestyle because I couldn't stand the thought of being tied to one location for the rest of my life[/24]. I love the freedom of just driving away when I get bored. But there are downsides. [29]The lack of a permanent address makes basic things like voting or receiving mail incredibly complicated[/29]. You don't realize how much society is built around having a fixed postcode until you don't have one anymore. It’s a constant administrative headache that takes a lot of planning to manage. || Speaker 5: I recently moved into a yurt in a communal forest. [25]I was looking for a way to live that fostered a deeper sense of connection with other people[/25]. In my old apartment block, I didn't know a soul. Here, we share meals and chores. It’s a beautiful way to live, but [30]the complete lack of personal privacy can be quite overwhelming at times[/30]. The canvas walls are thin, and you can hear everything. You have to be very comfortable with your neighbors to make it work. Sometimes I find myself going for long walks just to have a thought to myself without someone asking if I want a cup of tea.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) each speaker's main reason for choosing their current home.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions it was a dream, but emphasizes the 'constant battle' of maintenance he took on.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 says they were 'priced out' of the traditional market and wanted to avoid debt.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 wanted to live off-grid and prove a zero-waste lifestyle is possible.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 didn't want to be 'tied to one location' and wanted the freedom to move.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 wanted a 'deeper sense of connection with other people'.

options.B: to be part of a community
options.C: to be closer to their workplace
options.H: to have the flexibility to travel

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions as a significant drawback or adjustment.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 finds that the ocean views provide a 'sense of perspective' that makes the work worth it.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 says the biggest adjustment was limiting 'physical possessions to the bare essentials'.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 finds the 'skeptical and sometimes judgmental attitude' of authorities challenging.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 mentions the 'constant administrative headache' of not having a permanent address.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 finds the 'complete lack of personal privacy' overwhelming.

options.A: the difficulty of heating the space
options.B: the intrusion on their personal space
options.D: a renewed sense of life's proportions
options.E: the physical danger of the location
options.F: adopting a more minimalist way of life
options.H: the high cost of specialized repairs
